item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 2019 here, where did it come from?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Something about this article is just great, to me. It's like something straight out of a pulp horror magazine from the 40s, but updated with a much more modernized aesthetic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the idea. It at least to me, sounds original and that's appreciated in an article that uses a firey skeleton.

I would like to see specific references to what occult names, practices, or traditions can be linked to SCP-060-Alpha. If Mr. Corhill was able to research it, the Foundation should have an idea of what this thing might be. That said, Corhill could have discovered, created, or become (as I believe is implied) something completely new… So… Whatever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: He is the Lorax he speaks for the mother fucking trees

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The goofy occultness of the slack-jawed skeleton more than outweighs the negatives in tone and story. Love this thing, I want to see a Project Crossover story where Hellboy and 060-Alpha duke it out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
why can't they just uproot all the trees, dump them at the bottom of the ocean then set them on fire via oil

Because the Foundation's motto is "Secure, Contain, Protect", not "Destroy, Destroy, Destroy".

Seems like a Euclid at best

Object classes do not indicate how hard it is to destroy an object. They indicate how hard it is to contain it. This one is a Keter because its containment procedures are complicated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe i missed something but why can't they just uproot all the trees, dump them at the bottom of the ocean then set them on fire via oil or some other anomalous means? it already says you can destroy the skeleton with enough water and only ne appears at a time. you could even just remove all vegetation from around it then cover the whole are in asbestos followed by a massive bonfire. sure they'd be losses but so long as it wasn't able to reach another tree you could neutralise this one pretty easy. Seems like a Euclid at best

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Due to the extreme temperatures produced by 060-Alpha during the initial stages of manifestation, along with its physical capabilities, it is capable of causing large, uncontrolled fires and widespread property damage if left unchecked.

I think this line makes it keter: not so much because of the damage, but because it has no upper range limit and wildfires tend to attract attention. Combine that with the fact that these things go up like kindling at the smallest spark and you've got something that's a real pain in the rear to contain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This should be Euclid not keter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe this is just a hella old SCP, but I feel like this could be better. A lot of weird information and specifics in here that come across as very dated quality wise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It sounds cooler.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The link to SCP-2263 feels uncessary, as the to articles don't have any relation aside from having skeletons in them. It doesn't connect with the rest of the article.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired: Edit made. This may be reverted by the author at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 72 hours if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
- hide block

For this article, I'd like to propose a crosslink to SCP-2263, which denotes human skeletons comprised of anomalous materials. There is one entry in SCP-2263 that says to, "Keep away from open flame", and in SCP-060, the entity SCP-060-Alpha is an animate skeleton that is on fire.

Editing this passage:

When burned, SCP-060 will produce an entity henceforth designated SCP-060-Alpha. SCP-060-Alpha appears to be an animate adult human skeleton standing approximately 2.3 metres tall and surrounded by bright white flames.

To read:

When burned, SCP-060 will produce an entity henceforth designated SCP-060-Alpha. SCP-060-Alpha appears to be an animate adult human skeleton standing approximately 2.3 metres tall and surrounded by bright white flames.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cool SCP and all, and don't worry, I'm gonna upvote; but just backing up that A) This should probs be a Euclid rather than a Keter, and B) The entity should surely be -01 rather than -Alpha?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Would suggest changing to "one". That seems more fitting… somehow. But not my call.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this concept, but a minor question: Why is the skeleton designated SCP-060-Alpha, rather than -01 like other, similar entities in other entries?

The isolated, occult backstory is very cool.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thought this one was pretty funny.

Like, THE HELLISH FLAMING OCCULT SKELETON OF HELL!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It seems alright to me. I've seen similar wordings in professional works; I think it indicates something like "a person by the name of Johnathan Corhill" or "an individual named Johnathan Corhill".

The wordings is a little on the colloquial side, but it serves to let the reader know that this Johnathan Corhill wasn't particularly well-known individual and the information on said individual is not extensive.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"the house's previous owner was a Johnathan Corhill"

Grammatical error here? Regardless, this article is interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A skeleton from hell…unsettling and scary +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For some reason the title and the skeleton image makes me giggle what is WRONg with me??? This doesn't really scare me tbh, but I can see why it could, and I'm not one of those people who think that scary = good scp. +1 love the skeleton

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1 I saw this on GemoDawn's reading. I skipped the part about Jonathan, but with the code name having Infernal1 could mean that Jonathan summoned a spooky scary hellish skeleton.

Footnotes
1. And by google means: of, relating to, or characteristic of hell or the underworld.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's not a tie. I think you're misinterpreting it for the sternum of the rib cage.

item.title: jesus christ
item.content:encoded:
Mother of God… the foundation must be real how else could they capture this video of it?
http://m.youtube.com/watch?v=XTgFtxHhCQ0

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just noticed that SCP-60 Alpha is wearing a tie in that picture… XD

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not sure what to think of this one. The story is good, but could use some revision. I think it should be Euclid instead of Keter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not to necropost, but I find it somehow appropriate that the skeleton has a thread that will not die…
I too would like to see this listed as a possibility, even if in a dismissive tone. Too often, I feel like SCPs have gaps that any appropriately informed ( be that in science, occultism, history, etc.) reader can fill in. They make for fun puzzles (and are often my favorites), but the files should have better analysis. A good redaction or unavailable clearance-locked document could leave the mystery there for the real-world reader, while better replicating what the in-setting document would include.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The use of 107 in the destruction of 060 might have some unforeseen consequences. Unless controlled testing is preformed, we have no idea if the death of the 060 instances en mass would incite a new, stronger, 060-Alpha with intention to avenge the destruction to the surrounding area.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now that I think about it…

The use ofSCP-107 would be flawless in order to destroy SCP-60 with a right procedure in case of potential breach or any other bad scenario, don't you think?

Or am I wrong?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted. Only criticism is that it should be Euclid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Narrative Casuality is what powers many supersitions, supernatural and paranormal happenings. I agree with Anaxagoras that it should be at least mentioned as one of possibilities.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this one, mainly because it makes me wonder, is the burning skeleton the missing Johnathan Corhill? +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I always thought having both made more sense. With both present, nobody would need to convert anything. With only metric present, Americans who work for the Foundation would need to convert the numbers into imperial, at least initially. Why take the risk of somebody messing up?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm guessing something in it lit up for you?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I've warmed up enough to this one to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In that case, it's maybe Euclid, as the secondary effects still have a chance of occurring despite the best safeguards? Yes, maybe?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 50 miles = 80.4672 kilometers, so just above 80.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That would be reasonable, except that there is no mention of uncontained instances, known or presumed, and no instructions to be on the look out for them. The first few lines seem to imply that all the manifesting trees are known and greenhouse-enclosed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In a box, it wouldn't manifest at all, because it needs an outside ignition source.
It needs weekly maintenance by a gardener, who wouldn't even necessarily need to know about its nature.
Therefore, Euclid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Here's how I answered that question for myself when I asked it.

These things, from what I gather, can occur in the wild. Forest fires occur in the wild. There is a chance, however minuscule, that the flaming skeleton of death can manifest pretty much anywhere, and that's bad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why is it Keter?

Uncertain what I think overall. I tentatively like it, but holding off on a vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aha! Well. I'm glad it worked out!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Second article I've done that for :) .

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Groovy. At a high enough temperature, water breaks down into its elements of hydrogen and oxygen, which continue to feed a fire. 1500C would not be a sufficient temperature.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm glad I wasn't the only one to think this. Which isn't to say that I don't like the article!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it.

This one seems like a video game enemy too, but it holds up on its own. Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i have no idea what that means

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If we just lock it up, it would burn through whatever we have and go on to create infinite spawn points. The only way to contain it is through constant maintenance, therefore Keter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very good. I had to research this to make sure you got it right but the fire does indeed burn below the point of water's thermolysis into hydrogen and oxygen. Either you got that right by accident or did your homework.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Via what mechanism? With what evidence?

Remember: The Foundation is a scientific organization: They are not apt to use the laws of Narrative Causality to make such an assumption. While it is plausible, with no evidence at all (other than people vaugely mentioning he was interested in Victorian occultism), there'd be no reason to suspect that. After all, hundreds of thousands of people dabble in the occult without creating burning skeleton summoning trees.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fuck yes, this is creepy as hell.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Bitch i'm FABULOUS!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's pretty obvious that Corhill became 060-Alpha, so the fact that that possibility isn't even brought up does seem a little off. Decent SCP, but doesn't really grab me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this, but there are more than a few tonal issues in the Addendum. Not enough to prevent an upvote, but worthy of note.

finding it instead as a burned out shell.

cut off all ties

Later in the year

A few of those are probably just personal pet peeves more than real issues.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The grove is spread across approximately 8 acres in rural northeastern Minnesota

If you enjoyed the Acre™, why not try the Hectare™ from our Metric Units range? For this week only, take advantage of our Special Discount Offer: get 3.24 hectares for the price of 8 acres!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And then a skeleton popped out?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Looks nice now-kind of like it's showing off.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: badumtsh

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aren't you from Canada?
